executiveSummary: Mass-casualty disasters are ongoing: Venezuela’s earthquake death toll reportedly exceeds 5,000 with severe recovery needs; Europe’s late‑June heat wave is linked to >10,000 excess deaths. Flash flooding in Texas caused at least two deaths and >200 rescues across multiple counties; additional fatal flooding struck the U.S. Northeast. Landslides killed at least eight and left 34 missing in Chongqing, China; camp communities in Cox’s Bazar, Bangladesh suffered deadly slides during heavy rain. Wildfires continue under extreme heat: 12–13 fatalities in southern Spain; evacuations and transport disruption near Paris; smoke-driven health risks across the U.S. Midwest–Northeast. Severe floods continue to impact vulnerable populations, including 594,000+ affected in Mozambique; Missouri camp evacuations highlight U.S. Midwest flash‑flood exposure. Key Risk Drivers Compounding heat–wildfire nexus across Europe. High soil saturation and river response in Texas maintaining flash‑flood potential. Monsoon-season landslide/flood hazards in South and East Asia. Health-system strain from heat and smoke exposure across large populations. Assessment horizon Next 72 hours Confidence (overall) High for current impacts; Moderate for near‑term outlook

outlook72h: U.S. Gulf Coast: Tropical storm approaching Florida Panhandle and Alabama Gulf Coast may bring heavy rain, storm surge, and localized flooding; coastal preparedness (sandbagging, surge zones) underway. Texas Hill Country/South Texas: Saturated soils and elevated river stages sustain flash‑flood potential during any additional heavy rainfall. Northeastern U.S.: Intermittent heavy rain may trigger localized flash flooding where soils remain wet. Europe: Heat episodes keep wildfire risk elevated and continue to strain health services, especially among vulnerable groups. South/East Asia: Rain‑saturated slopes pose further landslide risk; response operations continue in Chongqing and Cox’s Bazar.
regionalTrends: Texas flood cluster with repeated rescues and declarations; localized deadly flooding in the Northeast.
North American wildfire smoke continues to degrade air quality; remote-area evacuations in Minnesota.
Peru earthquake with fatalities and displacement; Venezuela quake with mass casualties and prolonged relief needs.

Fatal wildfire in southern Spain; evacuations and transport disruption near Paris.
Mozambique flooding affecting >594,000 people across multiple provinces.
Monsoon-driven landslide and flood risk elevated in South/East Asia; deadly landslide in Chongqing and in Cox’s Bazar camps.
Tropical Storm Maysak flooding impacts in southern China with dozens of deaths.
rawMarkdown: Global Disaster Intelligence Brief — Open Source Intelligence Assessment Report type: Open Source Intelligence Assessment Escalate Monitor Ignore Overall Threat HIGH Global Disaster Intelligence Brief Tuesday, 21 July 2026 Executive Summary Mass-casualty disasters are ongoing: Venezuela’s earthquake death toll reportedly exceeds 5,000 with severe recovery needs; Europe’s late‑June heat wave is linked to >10,000 excess deaths. Flash flooding in Texas caused at least two deaths and >200 rescues across multiple counties; additional fatal flooding struck the U.S. Northeast. Landslides killed at least eight and left 34 missing in Chongqing, China; camp communities in Cox’s Bazar, Bangladesh suffered deadly slides during heavy rain. Wildfires continue under extreme heat: 12–13 fatalities in southern Spain; evacuations and transport disruption near Paris; smoke-driven health risks across the U.S. Midwest–Northeast. Severe floods continue to impact vulnerable populations, including 594,000+ affected in Mozambique; Missouri camp evacuations highlight U.S. Midwest flash‑flood exposure. Key Risk Drivers Compounding heat–wildfire nexus across Europe. High soil saturation and river response in Texas maintaining flash‑flood potential. Monsoon-season landslide/flood hazards in South and East Asia. Health-system strain from heat and smoke exposure across large populations. Assessment horizon Next 72 hours Confidence (overall) High for current impacts; Moderate for near‑term outlook Threat Dashboard Active Priority Incidents ESCALATE: 13 Multi-region, multi-hazard Watch List MONITOR: 2 Notable but subcritical Hazard Mix (ESCALATE) Flood/Flash Flood: 5 Wildfire/Smoke: 3 Earthquake: 2 Landslide: 2 Extreme Heat: 1 Populations at Highest Risk Children and displaced families (Mozambique; Cox’s Bazar) Elderly/medically vulnerable (Europe heat) Rural/riverine residents (Texas, Missouri) Remote visitors (Minnesota wilderness) Top Five Critical Events Venezuela — Major Earthquake, >5,000 deaths reported ESCALATE Mass fatalities; protracted emergency aid; severe shelter, WASH, and medical needs. Sources: Al Jazeera (18 Jul) · UNICEF (24 Jun) Europe — Extreme Heat, >10,000 excess deaths estimated ESCALATE High health-system demand; elderly and medically vulnerable most at risk. Source: AP News (17 Jul) Texas Hill Country & South Texas, USA — Life‑threatening Flash Flooding ESCALATE At least 2 deaths; >200 rescues; evacuations; multiple disaster declarations; rivers and low-water crossings hazardous. Sources: AP News (16 Jul) · AP News (15 Jul) · The Guardian (16 Jul) · CNN Transcript (16 Jul) Pengshui County, Chongqing, China — Deadly Landslide ESCALATE 8 dead; 34 missing; >1,100 evacuated; utilities disrupted; multiple structures buried/damaged. Sources: AP News (17 Jul) · Al Jazeera (17 Jul) Mozambique — Multi‑province Flooding, 594,000+ affected ESCALATE 306,000+ children among those affected; displacement concerns and operational strain ongoing. Source: UNICEF Flash Update (Date: Unknown) Humanitarian Impact Catastrophic mortality in Venezuela and Europe (heat) underscores urgent health, shelter, and WASH requirements. U.S. flash floods: rapid-onset rescues, evacuations, and fatalities in Texas and the Northeast highlight high exposure along rivers and low-water crossings. Children and refugees disproportionately affected: >306,000 children in Mozambique’s floods; deadly slides in Cox’s Bazar refugee camps. Wildfire impacts spanning evacuation, injury, and displacement: 12–13 fatalities in southern Spain; evacuations near Paris; smoke-related respiratory risk across U.S. regions. Infrastructure Concerns Road washouts, highway closures, and submerged intersections in Texas; isolation of communities and constrained ingress/egress. Structural collapse (Missouri campground building); widespread quake damage in Venezuela; building damage in Peru. Chongqing slide cut water/electricity/gas; multiple structures buried/damaged. Rail and motorway disruption near Paris due to wildfire; air-quality related service constraints across smoke-affected U.S. regions. Regional and Hazard Trends Americas Texas flood cluster with repeated rescues and declarations; localized deadly flooding in the Northeast. North American wildfire smoke continues to degrade air quality; remote-area evacuations in Minnesota. Peru earthquake with fatalities and displacement; Venezuela quake with mass casualties and prolonged relief needs. Europe Persistent extreme heat linked to high excess mortality; elevated wildfire risk. Fatal wildfire in southern Spain; evacuations and transport disruption near Paris. Africa & Asia Mozambique flooding affecting >594,000 people across multiple provinces. Monsoon-driven landslide and flood risk elevated in South/East Asia; deadly landslide in Chongqing and in Cox’s Bazar camps. Tropical Storm Maysak flooding impacts in southern China with dozens of deaths. Next 72‑Hour Outlook U.S. Gulf Coast: Tropical storm approaching Florida Panhandle and Alabama Gulf Coast may bring heavy rain, storm surge, and localized flooding; coastal preparedness (sandbagging, surge zones) underway. Texas Hill Country/South Texas: Saturated soils and elevated river stages sustain flash‑flood potential during any additional heavy rainfall. Northeastern U.S.: Intermittent heavy rain may trigger localized flash flooding where soils remain wet. Europe: Heat episodes keep wildfire risk elevated and continue to strain health services, especially among vulnerable groups. South/East Asia: Rain‑saturated slopes pose further landslide risk; response operations continue in Chongqing and Cox’s Bazar. Recommended Monitoring Priorities Venezuela earthquake: track casualty updates, aid access, WASH/health surveillance, and displacement dynamics. Texas: real‑time river gauges (e.g., Guadalupe, Pedernales), flash‑flood warnings, shelter capacities, and high‑water closure maps. Europe heat: mortality surveillance, hospital load, cooling‑center coverage, and wildfire watch indices. Mozambique: logistical access, shelter and WASH gaps, and disease outbreak early warning (cholera, diarrheal disease). Chongqing landslide: search‑and‑rescue tempo, missing persons status, utility restoration timelines, slope stability under continued rain. Cox’s Bazar: slope stabilization, drainage, relocations for highest‑risk shelters, child protection support. North American smoke: AQI trends, respiratory ER visits, targeted advisories for sensitive groups. Gulf Coast storm: NHC advisories, surge-prone zones, localized flash‑flood triggers, and power outage probabilities. Event Cards by Decision Level ESCALATE Earthquake — Venezuela ESCALATE People impact: Reported death toll >5,000; significant child protection, shelter, WASH, and medical needs. Infrastructure: Widespread structural collapse and prolonged emergency aid operations. Uncertainty: Figures may evolve as recovery proceeds. Sources: Al Jazeera (18 Jul) · UNICEF (24 Jun) Extreme Heat — Multiple countries in Europe ESCALATE People impact: Excess mortality may exceed 10,000; elderly and medically vulnerable at highest risk. Infrastructure: Health system strain; heat is the primary driver rather than physical damage. Source: AP News (17 Jul) Flash Floods — Texas Hill Country & South Texas, USA ESCALATE 2+ deaths 200+ rescues Evacuations People impact: At least two fatalities; widespread water rescues and evacuations across multiple counties. Reporting varies by source and locality. Infrastructure: Washed‑out highways, submerged intersections, rising rivers (e.g., Guadalupe, Pedernales), disaster declarations across affected areas. Sources: AP News (16 Jul) · AP News (15 Jul) · The Guardian (16 Jul) · CNN Transcript (16 Jul) Floods — Missouri, USA ESCALATE 200+ airlifted Camp building collapse People impact: >200 children and staff airlifted from a summer camp; additional rescues reported. Infrastructure: Camp facility collapse; state emergency response measures activated. Sources: The Guardian (11 Jul) · The Guardian (10 Jul) Floods — Mozambique (multi‑province) ESCALATE 594,681 affected 306,000+ children People impact: Large affected population with ongoing displacement concerns and needs for essential services. Infrastructure: Strained service delivery and humanitarian operations across several provinces. Source: UNICEF Flash Update (Date: Unknown) Landslide — Pengshui County, Chongqing, China ESCALATE 8 dead 34 missing 1,100+ evacuated People impact: Fatalities, missing persons, and evacuations following homes buried by slides. Infrastructure: Multiple buildings affected; water, electricity, and gas disrupted in the area. Sources: AP News (17 Jul) · Al Jazeera (17 Jul) Landslides & Flash Floods — Rohingya Camps, Cox’s Bazar, Bangladesh ESCALATE People impact: Fatalities, including children; highly vulnerable displaced population at risk. Infrastructure: Camp conditions destabilized; humanitarian partners coordinating emergency response. Source: UNICEF (9 Jul) Wildfire — Fontainebleau, near Paris, France ESCALATE People impact: Residential evacuations due to wildfire threat during a regional heat emergency. Infrastructure: Disrupted train lines; partial closure of A6 motorway. Sources: AP News (13 Jul) · Al Jazeera (13 Jul) Wildfire — Almería Province, Southern Spain ESCALATE 12–13 deaths (reports vary) 1,400+ evacuated People impact: Multiple fatalities; burn injuries reported; evacuations across affected communities. Infrastructure: >6,000 hectares burned; homes and communities threatened. Uncertainty: Fatality counts vary across reporting cycles. Sources: The Guardian (10 Jul) · The Guardian (12 Jul) · AP News (15 Jul) Earthquake — Near Sicaya, Peru ESCALATE People impact: At least six dead; >30 injured; ~300 displaced. Infrastructure: Building collapses in affected area. Source: The Guardian (19 Jul) Flash Floods — Northeastern United States ESCALATE People impact: At least two deaths; hundreds of rescues during torrential rain. Infrastructure: Flash‑flood warnings and flooding across multiple communities. Source: The Guardian (18 Jul) Flooding — Southern China (Tropical Storm Maysak) ESCALATE People impact: 39 deaths reported. Infrastructure: Mud and debris require extensive cleanup and disinfection. Source: Al Jazeera (9 Jul) Wildfire & Smoke — Minnesota wilderness; Midwest–Northeast U.S.; Ontario, Canada ESCALATE Evacuations Unhealthy air quality People impact: Thousands evacuated from a remote Minnesota wilderness; unhealthy smoke exposure across multiple U.S. states. Infrastructure: Remote access constraints complicate evacuation; broad air‑quality degradation affects daily life. Sources: AP News (15 Jul) · AP News (18 Jul) · Investing.com (18 Jul) MONITOR Tropical Storm — Florida Panhandle & Alabama Gulf Coast, USA MONITOR Outlook: Heavy rain, storm surge, and damaging wind possible; preparedness actions (sandbags, coastal advisories) underway. Action: Track NHC advisories; monitor surge zones and low‑lying communities. Source: AP News (21 Jul) Earthquake — Chiapas–Guatemala Border Region MONITOR Impact: Evacuations and limited injuries reported; no major structural damage confirmed. Action: Monitor aftershocks, landslide‑prone corridors, and cross‑border impact on services. Source: AP News (17 Jul) Heatwave — United States MONITOR Outlook: Prolonged dangerous heat and elevated overnight temperatures increase health risk for millions. Action: Expand cooling access and risk communications for sensitive groups. Source: AP News (11 Jul) IGNORE No IGNORE‑level incidents designated this cycle. Items previously labeled “ignore” were consolidated into monitoring where situational awareness remains warranted.
